---
title: "Network conservation and equivalent circuits"
description: "Derive node and loop analysis from conservation, reduce linear one-ports to Thévenin and Norton equivalents, and verify their predictions."
chapter-id: F04
status: draft
---

::: {.callout-note title="Chapter maturity — draft"}
This draft includes the chapter's derivations, worked examples, computational
checks, and exercises. Aligned Lab **L03** supplies the physical observations.
See the [reading roadmap](../roadmap.qmd) for the meaning of status levels.
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Safety boundary for this chapter"}
Practical work discussed here is limited to **current-limited,
extra-low-voltage** DC networks under local supervision and an approved procedure.
Do not determine a short-circuit current by physically shorting an unknown source.
De-energize before changing topology or measuring resistance. This chapter is not
authorization for mains, high voltage, high-energy cells or battery packs, RF
power, rotating machines, medical equipment, or safety-critical systems. Apply
the source–path–target controls of [F01](f01-safe-practice.qmd) [@iec61010].
:::

## Central question

> How can conservation laws predict every node voltage and branch current in a
> connected network?

The word *every* needs a boundary. This chapter treats connected, lumped,
steady-DC networks whose resistors are linear over the stated range and whose
sources have consistent terminal relations. Kirchhoff's laws supply conservation
constraints; the element relations from [F03](f03-sources-loads-resistance.qmd)
supply the rest. Together, and only together, they can determine the operating
point.

Consider the bridge in @fig-f04-bridge. Two unloaded dividers would put TP1 near
$6.67$ V and TP2 near $6.88$ V. Connecting $R_5$ makes them interact. Does bridge
current $I_{12}$ flow in its drawn reference direction, from TP1 to TP2, or the
other way? Is it closer to $30~\mu\text{A}$, $3$ mA, or $300$ mA? A negative
answer later will not mean the calculation failed; it will mean the physical
current opposes the reference chosen before calculation.

![A five-resistor bridge used to connect schematic topology, node equations, branch currents, code, and test points. All quantities are steady DC values.](../../../build/circuits/f04-bridge.png){#fig-f04-bridge fig-alt="A 10 volt source feeds two resistor dividers. R1 and R2 meet at TP1 with voltage v1; R3 and R4 meet at TP2 with voltage v2; R5 joins TP1 to TP2. Bridge current I12 is referenced from TP1 toward TP2, and TP0 is the zero volt reference."}

::: {.callout-tip title="Predict before writing equations"}
Compare the two divider ratios first. If $R_5$ were absent, which midpoint would
be higher? Connecting a positive resistor cannot reverse that ordering without
some other source. Commit to the sign and order of magnitude of $I_{12}$. Then
ask what should happen in three limits: $R_5\to\infty$, $R_5\to0$, and equal
divider ratios $R_1/R_2=R_3/R_4$. These predictions become checks on the algebra.
:::

## Learning outcomes

This chapter assumes [F03](f03-sources-loads-resistance.qmd): declared voltage
and current references, ideal sources, linear resistance, series/parallel
reduction, loading, and steady-DC operating points. It uses only the algebra and
simultaneous-equation methods introduced in
[M01](../appendices/m01-algebra-units-complex.qmd). After completing it you
should be able to:

- **identify nodes, branches, paths, loops, meshes, and ports**, select a
  reference node without confusing it with protective earth, and declare all
  signed quantities before using them;
- **derive and apply Kirchhoff's current law (KCL) and Kirchhoff's voltage law
  (KVL) within the lumped-circuit boundary**, combining them with resistor and
  source relations to solve and verify one- and two-node networks, including
  Millman's shortcut for parallel source branches;
- **choose between node-voltage and planar mesh-current analysis**, including
  the supernode and supermesh extensions, and interpret a negative solution;
- **use superposition only for linear voltage and current responses**, deactivate
  independent ideal sources correctly, and explain why powers do not superpose;
- **derive and use Thévenin and Norton equivalents at a declared port**, while
  stating exactly which external behavior they preserve and which internal
  behavior they do not;
- **derive the resistive maximum-power condition and its efficiency cost**, then
  reject matching when voltage regulation or energy efficiency is the real
  requirement; and
- **plan a bounded network test** using named test points, instrument loading,
  tolerances, residuals, uncertainty limits, and an explicit decision rule.

## Topology says which equations are independent

A schematic is not a picture of spatial layout. It is a statement about
connectivity. The following vocabulary makes that statement precise:

- A **node** is a set of points connected by ideal wire and therefore assigned
  one potential in the lumped description.
- A **branch** is one circuit element, or a declared element combination,
  between two nodes. Its branch current has one chosen reference direction.
- A **path** is a sequence of connected branches that does not revisit a node.
- A **loop** is a closed path.
- A **mesh** is a loop containing no other loop inside it in a particular planar
  drawing. Every mesh is a loop; not every loop is a mesh.
- A **port** is a pair of terminals through which an external circuit interacts
  with a network. Port voltage and port current must be declared together.

The **reference node** is the node you assign $0$ V so that every other node
voltage has a common coordinate. This is an algebraic choice. The reference node
need not be earth, chassis, or protective earth, and current does not disappear
there. Choosing another reference shifts every node potential by one constant.
It leaves every branch voltage and current unchanged.

For a connected network with $n$ nodes and $b$ branches, there are $n-1$
independent node conservation equations and $b-n+1$ independent loop equations.
Other KCL or KVL equations are valid but algebraically redundant. The count does
not eliminate the need for $b$ branch relations: conservation says how branch
quantities fit together, while a resistor or source says what each branch does.
A compact topological argument makes the count recoverable. A **spanning tree**
connects all $n$ nodes without a loop and therefore contains $n-1$ branches. Each
of the remaining $b-(n-1)=b-n+1$ branches closes one independent loop when added
to that tree. Likewise, one node KCL equation is the negative sum of all the
others, leaving $n-1$ independent balances [@sedra2020microelectronic].

::: {.callout-note title="A repeatable solve-and-check routine"}
For every network in this chapter:

1. mark nodes, branches, and the operating condition;
2. choose a $0$ V reference and declare branch-current directions and voltage
   polarities;
3. estimate signs, magnitudes, and useful limits;
4. write only independent conservation equations and all required branch
   relations;
5. solve with units attached;
6. interpret negative results as reversal of the declared reference; and
7. substitute the result back into every node or loop equation and inspect the
   residual before trusting it.
:::

## Current balance is charge conservation with a boundary

Enclose a physical junction by a fixed surface. Define the surface normal as
outward and define each listed branch current as positive **into** the enclosed
region. Integral charge conservation gives the exact statement

$$
\sum_k I_{k,\mathrm{in}}(t)
=\frac{\mathrm d Q_{\mathrm{stored}}(t)}{\mathrm dt}.
$$ {#eq-f04-continuity}

Current has units C/s, as does $\mathrm dQ/\mathrm dt$, so the dimensions agree.
The conservation law in @eq-f04-continuity is not a resistor relation and does
not forbid
charge accumulation. An imbalance is precisely the rate at which charge inside
the boundary changes.

In the **lumped ideal-node approximation**, charge storage within the small node
region is negligible, or storage has been represented explicitly as a capacitor
branch. Then $\mathrm dQ_{\mathrm{stored}}/\mathrm dt\approx0$, and Kirchhoff's
current law (KCL) becomes

$$
\boxed{\sum_k I_{k,\mathrm{in}}=0.}
$$ {#eq-f04-kcl-lumped}

Equivalently, total current entering equals total current leaving. This is the
network form of the continuity result introduced in [F02](f02-charge-voltage-current.qmd#nodes-ideal-wires-and-the-reach-of-a-lumped-description).

For example, if declared currents of $2.00$ mA and $0.80$ mA enter a node and
one declared current $I_x$ leaves, KCL gives
$2.00+0.80-I_x=0$ mA, hence $I_x=2.80$ mA. If the solution were
$I_x=-2.80$ mA **after its arrow had instead been declared into the node**, the
negative result would mean $2.80$ mA actually leaves against that arrow. A sign
describes agreement with a reference; it is not a second physical current.

The leading correction is parasitic storage. An unrepresented node capacitance
$C_p$ carrying node voltage $v$ contributes $C_p\,\mathrm dv/\mathrm dt$. At
steady DC, $\mathrm dv/\mathrm dt=0$; during a fast transition, ignoring it can
make a resistor-only KCL prediction fail. F07 makes that branch explicit.

## Loop consistency follows declared potential changes

Choose a closed traversal $a\to b\to c\to a$. Define branch voltage
$v_{xy}\equiv V_x-V_y$ before assigning a sign. The signed potential changes
around the traversal telescope:

$$
(V_b-V_a)+(V_c-V_b)+(V_a-V_c)=0.
$$

Thus, for a lumped loop whose branch voltages are defined consistently,
Kirchhoff's voltage law (KVL) is

$$
\boxed{\sum_{\text{closed traversal}} \Delta v_k=0.}
$$ {#eq-f04-kvl-lumped}

This is an exact algebraic consequence of single-valued node potentials inside
the lumped description. Use this sign rule while traversing a branch:

- crossing from its marked $-$ terminal to $+$ is a rise and contributes $+v$;
- crossing from $+$ to $-$ is a drop and contributes $-v$.

Reversing every traversal sign gives the same physical solution.

The electromagnetic boundary matters. Give the loop an orientation and choose
its positive surface normal by the right-hand rule. Faraday's law states

$$
\oint_C \vec E\mathbin{\cdot}\mathrm d\vec l
=-\frac{\mathrm d\Phi_B}{\mathrm dt}.
$$ {#eq-f04-faraday}

Significant changing magnetic flux linked by the physical loop makes the electric
field path-dependent. A zero-sum calculation that includes only resistor drops
then omits the induced electromotive contribution. Lumped circuit analysis
remains useful when
geometry is electrically small and induced or stored-energy effects are
negligible at the timescale of interest, or when those effects are represented by
explicit inductive and coupled branches [@griffiths2017electrodynamics;
@ulaby2015electromagnetics]. All numerical examples below are steady DC resistive
networks, so the right side of @eq-f04-faraday is zero.

## Node voltages make current balance systematic

Node-voltage analysis is usually the first method to try because a circuit with
$n$ nodes needs only $n-1$ unknown node voltages. Choose node $0$ as the
reference. For a resistor $R_{km}$ from node $k$ to node $m$, declare branch
current $i_{km}$ positive from $k$ to $m$. The linear **component relation**, the
equation that connects the resistor's terminal voltage and current, is

$$
i_{km}=\frac{v_k-v_m}{R_{km}}
=G_{km}(v_k-v_m),
\qquad G_{km}\equiv\frac{1}{R_{km}}.
$$ {#eq-f04-branch-current}

At node $k$, take $i_{\mathrm{inj},k}$ as the algebraic sum of independent source
currents injected into the node. KCL then gives the reusable node equation

$$
\boxed{\sum_m G_{km}(v_k-v_m)=i_{\mathrm{inj},k}.}
$$ {#eq-f04-node-general}

Every term has units S·V = A. The left side lists resistor currents leaving; the
right side lists source current entering. That declared convention prevents a
memorized sign pattern from replacing reasoning.

### Millman's shortcut solves parallel source branches at once

An important special case of @eq-f04-node-general occurs when several linear
branches share one unknown node and the reference node. **Millman's theorem** is
the resulting one-equation nodal solution, not an additional conservation law.

Let the unknown node voltage be $v$ relative to the reference node. In branch
$k$, an ideal voltage source fixes a node at $V_k$ relative to the same reference,
and a series resistance $R_k>0$ connects that fixed-potential node to $v$. Define
the branch current positive from $v$ toward $V_k$, so

$$
i_k=\frac{v-V_k}{R_k}.
$$

Also define every independent current $J_m$ as positive when injected **into**
the unknown node. KCL—resistor currents leaving equal source currents entering—
gives

$$
\sum_k\frac{v-V_k}{R_k}=\sum_m J_m.
$$

Collecting the terms in $v$ produces Millman's relation:

$$
\boxed{
v=
\frac{\displaystyle\sum_k V_k/R_k+\sum_m J_m}
{\displaystyle\sum_k 1/R_k}
}.
$$ {#eq-f04-millman}

The numerator has units of current and the denominator has units of conductance,
so $[v]=\text{A}/\text{S}=\text{V}$. Source polarity is carried by the algebraic
sign of $V_k$; a current source pointing out of the node enters the numerator as
a negative $J_m$.

For a concrete check, connect an unknown node to $10.0$ V through $1.00$ kΩ and
to $0$ V through both $2.00$ kΩ and a $3.00$ kΩ load. Inject $1.00$ mA into the
node. These are the branch values later drawn in @fig-f04-one-port. Millman's
relation gives

$$
v=
\frac{10.0~\text{V}/(1.00~\text{k}\Omega)+1.00~\text{mA}}
{1/(1.00~\text{k}\Omega)+1/(2.00~\text{k}\Omega)
 +1/(3.00~\text{k}\Omega)}
=\frac{11.00~\text{mA}}{1.833~\text{mS}}
=6.000~\text{V}.
$$ {#eq-f04-millman-loaded}

Removing the $3.00$ kΩ load removes its conductance from the denominator and
gives $v=11.00~\text{mA}/1.500~\text{mS}=7.333$ V. This is the open-circuit
voltage found later by direct KCL.

The same calculation anticipates the one-port equivalent. For this class of
independent-source branches,

$$
G_\text{eq}=\sum_k\frac{1}{R_k},
\qquad
R_\text{th}=\frac{1}{G_\text{eq}},
\qquad
I_\text{N}=\sum_k\frac{V_k}{R_k}+\sum_m J_m,
\qquad
V_\text{th}=I_\text{N}R_\text{th}.
$$ {#eq-f04-millman-equivalent}

Thus Millman's numerator is the Norton current and its denominator is the
zero-source port conductance. For the open one-port above,
$R_\text{th}=1/(1.500~\text{mS})=666.7~\Omega$ and
$I_\text{N}=11.00$ mA, so $V_\text{th}=7.333$ V. The nodal shortcut and the
equivalent are two readings of the same linear terminal relation
[@sedra2020microelectronic].

The shortcut has a strict topology and validity range. All included branches must
connect the same node pair and have linear relations over the stated operating
range. A zero-resistance ideal voltage-source branch cannot be inserted into the
conductance sum; it directly constrains $v$, and multiple such sources must impose
compatible voltages. A bridge or general multi-node network still requires the
coupled node equations. Nonlinear devices, current limiting, and temperature-
dependent resistance also invalidate one fixed set of Millman coefficients.

### Worked example: node-voltage solution of a loaded bridge

In @fig-f04-bridge, TP0 is $0$ V and TP$_S$ is constrained by the ideal source to
$V_S=10.0$ V. Define $I_{12}$ positive from TP1 to TP2. KCL at TP1 and TP2 gives

$$
\frac{v_1-10.0}{1.00~\text{k}\Omega}
+\frac{v_1}{2.00~\text{k}\Omega}
+\frac{v_1-v_2}{4.70~\text{k}\Omega}=0,
$$ {#eq-f04-bridge-node1}

$$
\frac{v_2-10.0}{1.50~\text{k}\Omega}
+\frac{v_2}{3.30~\text{k}\Omega}
+\frac{v_2-v_1}{4.70~\text{k}\Omega}=0.
$$ {#eq-f04-bridge-node2}

Using volts and kilohms makes every current term a milliampere. Collecting terms
and solving the two simultaneous equations by elimination gives

$$
v_1=6.688~\text{V},
\qquad
v_2=6.841~\text{V}.
$$

The bridge-current reference was TP1 to TP2, so

$$
I_{12}=\frac{v_1-v_2}{R_5}
=\frac{6.688-6.841}{4.70~\text{k}\Omega}
=-32.6~\mu\text{A}.
$$ {#eq-f04-bridge-current}

The negative sign confirms that the actual current is $32.6~\mu\text{A}$ from TP2 to
TP1. It is close to the predicted tens-of-microamperes scale, not milliamperes.
At TP1, $3.312$ mA arrives through $R_1$, while $3.344$ mA leaves through $R_2$
and the declared bridge current is $-0.0326$ mA. The signed residual is
$3.312-3.344-(-0.0326)\approx0$ mA; the small displayed remainder is rounding.

The limiting cases teach more than another decimal place:

- $R_5\to\infty$ opens the bridge and recovers two independent dividers;
- $R_5\to0$ merges TP1 and TP2 into one node, so their voltage difference tends
  to zero while the finite branch-current limit must be found from the rest of
  the network rather than by writing $0/0$; and
- $R_1/R_2=R_3/R_4$ balances the bridge, giving $v_1=v_2$ and $I_{12}=0$ for any
  finite $R_5$ under the ideal assumptions.

Contact resistance, resistor self-heating, source resistance, and meter input
resistance are leading ways a physical bridge can depart from these values.

### A voltage source between unknown nodes creates a supernode

An ideal voltage source connected from an unknown node directly to the reference
fixes that node voltage and causes no difficulty. A voltage source between two
unknown nodes $p$ and $q$ does not provide its current as a function of voltage,
so one cannot replace its branch current with $(v_p-v_q)/R$.

Instead, surround both nodes and the source with one **supernode**. Write KCL for
all branches crossing the boundary, then add the source constraint. If the source
is marked $+$ at $p$, $-$ at $q$, and has value $V_X$,

$$
\sum I_{\text{leaving supernode}}=0,
\qquad v_p-v_q=V_X.
$$ {#eq-f04-supernode}

Those are two independent equations for the two node voltages. The current inside
the voltage-source branch can be recovered afterward from either node's KCL.

## Mesh currents organize planar loop equations

Mesh-current analysis is the planar dual of node-voltage analysis. It applies
only when the network can be drawn on a plane without branch crossings that are
not nodes. Assign one algebraic current to each mesh; these currents are solution
variables, not necessarily distinct physical branch currents. In a branch shared
by two meshes, the physical current is their signed difference when the mesh
references oppose.

In @fig-f04-two-mesh, define both $I_1$ and $I_2$ clockwise. The downward
reference through shared $R_2$ is therefore $I_1-I_2$. The left source is a
$10.0$ V rise when traversed clockwise. The right source has $+$ at its top, so
the clockwise right traversal crosses it as a $5.00$ V drop. KVL gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
(R_1+R_2)I_1-R_2I_2 &= V_1,\\
-R_2I_1+(R_2+R_3)I_2 &= -V_2.
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-f04-two-mesh-system}

![Two clockwise mesh-current references share $R_2$. The sign of the right-hand source term follows its marked polarity and the clockwise traversal.](../../../build/circuits/f04-two-mesh.png){#fig-f04-two-mesh fig-alt="Two adjacent planar loops share a vertical 2 kilohm resistor. The left loop has a 10 volt source and 1 kilohm resistor; the right has a 3 kilohm resistor and 5 volt source. Both mesh currents are clockwise, so the downward shared current is I1 minus I2."}

Substituting kilohms and volts, so the unknowns emerge in milliamperes,

$$
3I_1-2I_2=10,
\qquad -2I_1+5I_2=-5.
$$

Elimination gives

$$
I_1=3.636~\text{mA},
\qquad I_2=0.455~\text{mA},
$$

and the actual shared downward current is
$I_1-I_2=3.182$ mA. The exact values are $I_1=40/11$ mA and
$I_2=5/11$ mA, which return zero KVL residual before rounding. Substituting the
displayed three-decimal currents instead gives residuals of approximately
$-2$ mV and $+3$ mV. The shared-node voltage provides an independent
representation check:

$$
v_{\mathrm{TP1}}
=(I_1-I_2)R_2
=6.364~\text{V},
$$

which also equals $10.0~\text{V}-I_1R_1$ and
$5.00~\text{V}+I_2R_3$.

A current source shared by two meshes prevents writing a voltage drop for that
branch. Form a **supermesh** around the outer perimeter, omitting the current
source from the KVL traversal, and add its current constraint. For a source
$I_X$ directed with mesh 1 and against mesh 2 in the shared branch,
$I_1-I_2=I_X$. A current source on the outer boundary directly sets the
adjacent mesh current.

### Choosing between node-voltage and mesh-current analysis

| Situation | Usually efficient | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Clear series/parallel structure | direct reduction | fewest equations and clearest physical reading |
| Few essential nodes; many current sources | node voltage | roughly one unknown per non-reference node |
| Planar circuit; few meshes; many voltage sources | mesh current | roughly one unknown per mesh |
| Need each source's contribution | superposition | isolates linear cause while retaining the same network |
| Many candidate loads at one terminal pair | Thévenin/Norton | solve the internal network once, then vary the load |

An equation solver reporting a singular system is often a circuit clue. A floating
subnetwork may have no defined absolute potential and therefore infinitely many
node-coordinate solutions; contradictory ideal voltage sources may demand two
different voltages for the same nodes and yield no solution. Check connectivity,
reference choice, and source consistency before blaming arithmetic.

## Linearity lets source contributions add

A network is linear over a stated operating range when scaling every independent
excitation by $a$ scales a voltage or current response by $a$, and adding two
excitations adds their responses. Linear resistors and linear controlled sources
meet this requirement while their parameters remain constant. A diode, current
limit, saturating amplifier, temperature-dependent resistor, or changing switch
state generally does not.

A **controlled (dependent) source** is an ideal source whose value is fixed by a
voltage or current elsewhere in the same network—for example
$i_d=g_m v_x$. It can be linear even though it is not independent. When a test
voltage $v_t$ is applied at a port, the relation $i_d=g_m v_x(v_t)$ remains part
of the equations; deleting it would describe a different network. A02 develops
physical controlled-source behavior in detail.

The **superposition theorem** follows: in a linear network, any branch voltage or
current equals the algebraic sum of the responses produced by each independent
source acting alone. “Acting alone” means setting every other independent ideal
source to zero while retaining its zero-valued constitutive relation:

- $0$ V across an ideal voltage source is a short circuit;
- $0$ A through an ideal current source is an open circuit; and
- dependent sources, if present, remain active because their controlling
  relation is part of the network.

This is the standard linear-network statement of superposition
[@sedra2020microelectronic].

This is an analysis operation, not an instruction to manipulate a physical
supply. Internal or series resistance stays in the network.

Use the one-port network in @fig-f04-one-port with the $3.00$ kΩ load connected.
Define $v=V_A-V_B$ and load current $i$ positive leaving the network at A and
entering $R_L$. Direct KCL at A is

$$
\frac{v-10.0}{1.00~\text{k}\Omega}
+\frac{v}{2.00~\text{k}\Omega}
+\frac{v}{3.00~\text{k}\Omega}
-1.00~\text{mA}=0,
$$

which gives $v=6.000$ V and $i=2.000$ mA.

![The anchor one-port. Removing $R_L$ creates the open circuit used for $V_\text{th}$; replacing it by a safe calculated short condition defines $I_\text{N}$; reconnecting it checks the equivalent.](../../../build/circuits/f04-one-port.png){#fig-f04-one-port fig-alt="A 10 volt source feeds port A through a 1 kilohm resistor. A 2 kilohm resistor and a 1 milliamp source connect between A and zero volt terminal B. A removable 3 kilohm load connects A to B; load current leaves the network at A."}

With only $V_S$ active, the current source becomes an open circuit and
$v_{V_S}=60/11=5.455$ V. With only $I_S$ active, the voltage source becomes a
short circuit and the resistance from A to B is
$1.00\parallel2.00\parallel3.00=6/11$ kΩ, so

$$
v_{I_S}=(1.00~\text{mA})\left(\frac{6}{11}~\text{k}\Omega\right)
=0.545~\text{V}.
$$

Their sum is $6.000$ V, exactly the direct solution.

Power does **not** superpose because it is a product. For the load,
$P_L=v^2/R_L=12.0$ mW using the total voltage. Squaring the two separate
contributions and adding gives only $10.0$ mW; the missing cross-term
$2v_{V_S}v_{I_S}/R_L$ shows why adding source-by-source powers gives the wrong
total.

## Thévenin and Norton equivalents preserve linear port behavior

External circuitry connected only to A and B does not need every internal branch
value. It needs the relation between port voltage and port current. Continue to
define

$$
v\equiv V_A-V_B,
$$

and define $i$ positive **leaving** the source network at A and entering the load.
For a linear resistive one-port with a finite positive output resistance, its
affine terminal relation can be written

$$
\boxed{v=V_\text{th}-R_\text{th}i.}
$$ {#eq-f04-thevenin-line}

This form follows directly from superposition [@sedra2020microelectronic]. First
open the port, so $i=0$;
the internal independent sources produce $V_\text{OC}$. Next set those independent
sources to zero, retain all dependent sources, and drive the port with the declared
current $i$ leaving A. The linear zero-source network responds with a voltage
change $-R_\text{th}i$ under the chosen port references. Adding the two linear
responses gives $v=V_\text{OC}-R_\text{th}i$. Thus the Thévenin form is an ideal
voltage source $V_\text{th}=V_\text{OC}$ in series with $R_\text{th}$, not a
formula detached from superposition. At open circuit, $i=0$, so

$$
V_\text{th}=V_\text{OC}.
$$

Solving @eq-f04-thevenin-line for current gives

$$
\boxed{i=I_\text{N}-\frac{v}{R_\text{th}}},
\qquad
I_\text{N}\equiv\frac{V_\text{th}}{R_\text{th}}.
$$ {#eq-f04-norton-line}

This is the Norton form: an ideal current source $I_\text{N}$ directed from B to
A in parallel with $R_\text{th}$. At short circuit, $v=0$ and the current leaving
A is $I_\text{SC}=I_\text{N}$. Open circuit means zero port **current**, not zero
voltage; short circuit means zero port **voltage**, not zero current.

The units expose the conversion:
$[V_\text{th}/R_\text{th}]=\text{V}/\Omega=\text{A}$ and
$[I_\text{N}R_\text{th}]=\text{A}\cdot\Omega=\text{V}$.

### Methods for finding Thévenin and Norton port parameters

1. **Open-circuit voltage and safe analysis of short-circuit current.** Calculate
   $V_\text{OC}$ and $I_\text{SC}$, then
   $R_\text{th}=V_\text{OC}/I_\text{SC}$ when the ratio is defined. Do not
   physically short an unknown or energetic source.
2. **Deactivate independent sources and look into the port.** Replace independent
   ideal voltage sources by shorts and independent ideal current sources by opens;
   retain all resistances. This works for the resistor/independent-source networks
   in this chapter.
3. **Apply a test source.** Set independent sources to zero, retain every dependent
   source, mark the test voltage positive at A relative to B, and define
   $i_\text{test}$ positive **entering the deactivated network at A**. Then
   calculate $R_\text{th}=v_\text{test}/i_\text{test}$. Under the chapter's load
   current reference, $i_\text{test}=-i$. This is the general linear method
   once dependent sources appear. If a dependent-source network has no independent
   excitation, both $V_\text{OC}$ and $I_\text{SC}$ may be zero while the port
   resistance remains finite, so their ratio is useless.

For @fig-f04-one-port with $R_L$ removed, KCL gives

$$
\frac{v-10.0}{1.00~\text{k}\Omega}
+\frac{v}{2.00~\text{k}\Omega}
-1.00~\text{mA}=0,
$$

so

$$
V_\text{th}=V_\text{OC}=7.333~\text{V}.
$$

After suppressing the independent sources, the port sees

$$
R_\text{th}=R_1\parallel R_2
=\frac{(1.00)(2.00)}{1.00+2.00}~\text{k}\Omega
=0.667~\text{k}\Omega.
$$

Therefore

$$
I_\text{N}=I_\text{SC}
=\frac{7.333~\text{V}}{0.667~\text{k}\Omega}
=11.00~\text{mA}.
$$

Reconnect $R_L=3.00$ kΩ. The equivalent predicts

$$
i=\frac{V_\text{th}}{R_\text{th}+R_L}
=2.000~\text{mA},
\qquad
v=iR_L=6.000~\text{V},
$$ {#eq-f04-equivalent-loaded}

matching direct KCL and superposition. Three representations now agree at the
same named port.

::: {.callout-important title="Equivalent does not mean physically identical"}
Thévenin and Norton forms preserve the terminal $v$–$i$ relation for external
loads within the stated linear, steady-DC range. They do **not** preserve internal
node voltages, branch currents, component dissipation, noise, current limits,
stored energy, or ratings. The equivalent is port-specific: choosing another pair
of terminals generally gives different parameters. A nonlinear network may have
only a local small-signal equivalent around a named operating point.
:::

## Maximum resistive load power occurs at an equal-resistance match

Take a fixed resistive Thévenin source with $R_\text{th}>0$ and connect a variable
positive resistance $R_L$. Under the port references already declared,

$$
i=\frac{V_\text{th}}{R_\text{th}+R_L},
\qquad
P_L=i^2R_L
=\frac{V_\text{th}^2R_L}{(R_\text{th}+R_L)^2}.
$$ {#eq-f04-load-power}

The dimensions are
$[V^2R/R^2]=\text{V}^2/\Omega=\text{V}\cdot\text{A}=\text{W}$.
The limiting cases are sensible: $R_L\to0$ gives nearly zero load voltage and
therefore zero load power; $R_L\to\infty$ gives nearly zero current and again
zero load power. A maximum must lie between.

No calculus is required to locate it. Let $x=R_L/R_\text{th}>0$. Relative to the
candidate value at $x=1$,

$$
\frac{4x}{(1+x)^2}\le1
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
(1-x)^2\ge0.
$$

Equality holds only at $x=1$, so

$$
\boxed{R_L=R_\text{th}},
\qquad
\boxed{P_{L,\max}=\frac{V_\text{th}^2}{4R_\text{th}}}.
$$ {#eq-f04-max-power}

For the one-port above, the matched load is $0.667$ kΩ. Then

$$
v_L=\frac{V_\text{th}}{2}=3.667~\text{V},
\quad
i_L=5.50~\text{mA},
\quad
P_{L,\max}=20.2~\text{mW}.
$$

In the Thévenin source-plus-series-resistance representation, the same current
flows through $R_\text{th}$ and $R_L$. At the match their dissipations are equal,
so the port-delivery ratio

$$
\eta_\text{port}
=\frac{P_L}{P_L+P_{R_\text{th}}}
=50\%.
$$

That familiar 50% is **not automatically the overall efficiency of the original
multi-source network**, because the equivalent does not preserve internal power.
It is exact for a physical ideal voltage source with a real series resistance and
describes the Thévenin representation's source-to-load split. F05 develops full
power budgets.

Maximum power is therefore not the default goal for voltage delivery. If
$R_L\gg R_\text{th}$, load voltage stays near $V_\text{th}$ and the simple series
loss is small, although extracted power may be below its mathematical maximum.
Matching is useful when extracting maximum available signal or power is worth the
loss. Efficient regulated supplies instead aim for output resistance much smaller
than load resistance. Negative resistance, $R_\text{th}=0$, nonlinear limits, and
AC impedance matching lie outside this positive-resistive theorem; F08 develops
the frequency-domain case.

## Executable cross-check of the network solution

The following standard-library program solves the bridge's two node equations,
checks their residuals, and sweeps loads on the one-port. Parameters remain
visible, so changing a resistor changes the same quantities named in the prose
and schematics.

```python
def solve_2x2(a11, a12, a21, a22, b1, b2):
    """Solve A x = b for a nonsingular 2-by-2 system."""
    det = a11 * a22 - a12 * a21
    if abs(det) < 1e-18:
        raise ValueError("singular network equations")
    x1 = (b1 * a22 - a12 * b2) / det
    x2 = (a11 * b2 - b1 * a21) / det
    return x1, x2


def bridge_nodes(source_v, r1, r2, r3, r4, r5):
    g1, g2 = 1.0 / r1, 1.0 / r2
    g3, g4, g5 = 1.0 / r3, 1.0 / r4, 1.0 / r5
    v1, v2 = solve_2x2(
        g1 + g2 + g5, -g5,
        -g5, g3 + g4 + g5,
        g1 * source_v, g3 * source_v,
    )
    res1 = g1 * (v1 - source_v) + g2 * v1 + g5 * (v1 - v2)
    res2 = g3 * (v2 - source_v) + g4 * v2 + g5 * (v2 - v1)
    return v1, v2, (v1 - v2) / r5, res1, res2


def one_port_loaded(v_th, r_th, load_ohm):
    current_a = v_th / (r_th + load_ohm)
    return current_a * load_ohm, current_a


v1, v2, i12, res1, res2 = bridge_nodes(
    10.0, 1.00e3, 2.00e3, 1.50e3, 3.30e3, 4.70e3
)
print(f"bridge: v1={v1:.4f} V, v2={v2:.4f} V")
print(f"bridge: I12={i12 * 1e6:.2f} uA")
print(f"KCL residuals: {res1 * 1e9:.3f}, {res2 * 1e9:.3f} nA")

v_th = 22.0 / 3.0
r_th = 2.00e3 / 3.0
for load in (3.00e3, 1.00e3, r_th):
    voltage_v, current_a = one_port_loaded(v_th, r_th, load)
    print(
        f"RL={load:.1f} ohm: "
        f"v={voltage_v:.4f} V, i={current_a * 1e3:.4f} mA"
    )
```

Expected output:

```text
bridge: v1=6.6884 V, v2=6.8414 V
bridge: I12=-32.56 uA
KCL residuals: 0.000, 0.000 nA
RL=3000.0 ohm: v=6.0000 V, i=2.0000 mA
RL=1000.0 ohm: v=4.4000 V, i=4.4000 mA
RL=666.7 ohm: v=3.6667 V, i=5.5000 mA
```

The code independently repeats the arithmetic. Agreement with hand algebra
catches transcription and arithmetic errors, but it does not simulate non-ideal
hardware or measure a circuit. See @sec-evidence-workflow for the distinction.

## Load-sweep evidence for the linear one-port relation

The executable check confirms the arithmetic, but the linear one-port claim also
needs an observation that can challenge it. The network and its equivalent
predict a straight port relation, so a physical test should challenge that
prediction at several safe loads rather than celebrate one
agreeing point; this follows the prediction-first bench practice of
[@horowitz2015art]. For the one-port of @fig-f04-one-port, a bounded test would:

1. de-energize, confirm component values and load power ratings, and set the
   supply current limit;
2. declare $v=V_A-V_B$ with the red lead at TPA and black at TPB;
3. record the apparent open-circuit voltage with the voltmeter input resistance
   included—the meter means the physical port current is small, not exactly zero;
4. connect several approved loads, recording $R_L$, $v$, instrument range,
   source state, ambient condition, and raw digits;
5. derive total port current as
   $i_\text{port}=v/R_L+v/R_\text{in}$, with the first term omitted for the
   apparent-open row, and carry load tolerance, temperature, meter input
   resistance, and correlated voltage error into its bound; and
6. examine whether $v+R_\text{th}i_\text{port}$ remains consistent with one
   $V_\text{th}$.

The values in @tbl-f04-synthetic are **synthetic, illustrative data**, not an
instrument record. The first four rows were generated from
$V_\text{th}=7.333$ V and $R_\text{th}=666.7~\Omega$, with small illustrative
offsets. The last row deliberately imitates a source entering a current-limited
region. Assume, only for learning how to reason from the table, a 10 MΩ voltmeter
with ±1% input-resistance tolerance, an absolute voltage accuracy limit of ±5 mV
under the stated range, and load values within ±0.2% at $23\ ^\circ$C ±2 °C.
Those assumed limits are not a $k=2$ expanded uncertainty and do not qualify a
physical claim.

| $R_L$ | Synthetic indication $v$ | Derived total port current $i_\text{port}$ | Recorded source state | $v+(666.7~\Omega)i_\text{port}$ |
|---:|---:|---:|---|---:|
| apparent open | 7.335 V | 0.000734 mA | CV | 7.3355 V |
| 3.00 kΩ | 6.002 V | 2.0013 mA | CV | 7.3363 V |
| 1.00 kΩ | 4.398 V | 4.3984 mA | CV | 7.3304 V |
| 667 Ω | 3.665 V | 5.4951 mA | CV | 7.3286 V |
| 330 Ω | 2.200 V | 6.6669 mA | **current limit** | 6.6447 V |

: Synthetic load-sweep data generated for diagnosis. It can teach a residual test but cannot establish the behavior of physical hardware. {#tbl-f04-synthetic}

The first four inferred intercepts cluster within 8 mV and are visually
consistent with one line. A formal compatibility decision still requires
propagating the stated load, input-resistance, and voltage limits—including the
fact that the same voltage indication appears in both table columns. The final
row is hundreds of millivolts away and carries a state change, so even those
conservative limits cannot plausibly extend the same straight line into that
region. The bounded conclusion is that the synthetic pattern supports further
testing of one line over the first four points and falsifies its extension into
the indicated current-limit region; it qualifies no hardware.

A single mismatch has rivals. A wrong load value changes derived current; meter
loading lowers the apparent open-circuit voltage; lead/contact resistance changes
the loaded slope; resistor self-heating produces gradual curvature; a current
limit creates a knee. Record source indicators and measure at multiple separated
points because those hypotheses predict different patterns.

## Guarded acceptance decision for a port requirement

The load sweep tests the form of the port relation; an engineering decision must
also test that relation against a requirement. Suppose the one-port is intended
to drive an input guaranteed to have $R_L\ge3.00$ kΩ. The requirement is:

> For $V_S=10.0$ V ±1%, $I_S=1.00$ mA ±2%,
> $R_1=1.00$ kΩ ±1%, $R_2=2.00$ kΩ ±1%, and ambient conditions under which those
> limits apply, the steady-DC voltage $V_A-V_B$ shall be **strictly greater than
> 5.75 V** for every $R_L\ge3.00$ kΩ.

The matched load $R_L=R_\text{th}\approx667~\Omega$ fails immediately: it gives
only $3.667$ V nominally. Maximum available power is irrelevant because this is a
voltage-interface requirement.

For the stated linear network, the loaded voltage is

$$
v=\frac{V_S/R_1+I_S}
{1/R_1+1/R_2+1/R_L}.
$$ {#eq-f04-port-design}

The lowest load resistance is the worst load. A corner evaluation using
$V_S=9.90$ V, $I_S=0.980$ mA, $R_L=3.00$ kΩ, and all four $R_1,R_2$ tolerance
corners gives the minimum at $R_1=1.010$ kΩ and $R_2=1.980$ kΩ:

$$
v_\text{corner}=5.897~\text{V}.
$$

The calculated worst-corner margin under the listed assumptions is therefore
$5.897-5.750=0.147$ V. This is a worst-corner calculation under the listed
inputs, not proof that manufactured hardware passes: it omits temperature drift,
source output resistance, connector drops, and measurement effects unless their
limits are already included.

An acceptance test must say what a pass means. One suitable draft rule is:

- **Measurand and points:** steady $V_A-V_B$, red at TPA and black at TPB.
- **Conditions:** approved current-limited extra-low-voltage source and a
  qualification fixture whose guarded values are no more favorable than the
  calculated low-output corner: $V_S\le9.90$ V,
  $I_S\le0.980$ mA, $R_L\le3.00$ kΩ,
  $R_1\ge1.010$ kΩ, and $R_2\le1.980$ kΩ. Include stimulus and component-value
  uncertainty when establishing those inequalities, insert the actual fixture
  values into @eq-f04-port-design, record the declared ambient range, and wait
  for a stated steady condition. This deliberately severe qualification fixture
  is distinct from an end-item acceptance screen; using the latter requires a
  justified correlation to the qualification corner.
- **Instrument intrusion:** voltmeter input at least 10 MΩ, placed in parallel
  with $R_L$ in the prediction; record range, accuracy specification, calibration
  status, and leads.
- **Uncertainty basis:** establish an expanded uncertainty $U$ for the complete
  result using the method of F06; do not relabel a datasheet accuracy limit as
  $k=2$ uncertainty [@jcgm2008gum].
- **Guard band and rule:** reserve 50 mV beyond the strict requirement and accept
  only if $v_\text{indicated}-U>5.80$ V. Equality does not pass. Any current-limit
  indication, thermal drift outside the declared stability criterion, or result
  outside the predicted tolerance envelope is a stop and investigation condition.

This rule deliberately consumes part of the calculated 147 mV worst-corner
margin. Whether the remaining margin is sufficient cannot be decided until $U$
and omitted physical
effects are bounded. One end-item acceptance test also cannot demonstrate every
manufacturing corner unless component values are screened or separately bounded;
corner coverage belongs in design qualification and production controls. The
calculation therefore supports the test design but does not verify manufactured
hardware.

## Validity limits of lumped linear DC network analysis

The methods depend on their element relations and lumped-circuit assumptions.
Their main failure boundaries are:

- **Unrepresented storage or coupling:** parasitic capacitance permits transient
  node accumulation; changing linked flux adds induced emf. Add the relevant
  branches and use F07/F08 rather than insisting a resistor-only DC equation fit.
- **Nonlinear or changing elements:** diodes, saturating devices, heating
  resistors, switching states, and source current limits change the element
  relation. Piecewise or local analysis must name its operating region.
- **Non-ideal interconnect:** wire and contact resistance split what the schematic
  called one node. Measure both ends or add explicit branches.
- **Instrument intrusion:** finite voltmeter input resistance becomes another
  branch; ammeter burden changes a loop. Analyze the circuit-plus-instrument.
- **Floating or inconsistent ideal constraints:** an unreferenced island has an
  arbitrary absolute potential; opposing ideal voltage sources may have no
  simultaneous solution.
- **Overinterpreted reduction:** a port equivalent predicts external terminal
  behavior, not internal temperature, ratings, noise, or safety.

For each failure, compare the physical assembly, schematic topology, branch
relation, equation, code, and measurement. The first inconsistent description
usually determines the next diagnostic test.

## Network analysis in practice

Kirchhoff's laws are conservation constraints, not isolated recipes. KCL comes
from charge continuity after node accumulation is made negligible or explicit;
KVL comes from consistent potential changes after linked induction is negligible
or represented. Element relations complete the equations.

Network analysis starts by declaring references and estimating the expected
signs and magnitudes. Independent node or mesh equations determine the solution;
residuals and limiting cases then test it. Linearity allows independent source
responses to add. At a named port, a Thévenin or Norton relation preserves the
external voltage–current behavior across loads. Maximum power occurs at a
resistive match, but the voltage and loss consequences must still serve the
actual requirement.

Storage, frequency dependence, and nonlinear devices change the branch relations,
but they do not remove the conservation equations. The `Connections` section
below lists those extensions.

## Exercises

Declare references, operating conditions, units, and assumptions. Estimate before
calculating, and substitute solutions back into the governing equations. Full
solutions and assessment variants remain outside the public book; selected
self-check values are included for calibration.

### Quick check

One best answer each; the key follows.

1. A solved branch current is $-2.0$ mA. This means:
    a. current cannot be negative, so the network has no solution;
    b. the actual current is 2.0 mA opposite the declared arrow;
    c. KCL has failed;
    d. the resistor generated charge.
2. The node chosen as $0$ V is:
    a. necessarily protective earth;
    b. necessarily the path carrying all current;
    c. a potential reference that may or may not be earth;
    d. forbidden from connecting to a source.
3. To deactivate an independent ideal source for superposition:
    a. open a voltage source and short a current source;
    b. remove every source and its internal resistance;
    c. short a zero-valued voltage source and open a zero-valued current source;
    d. deactivate dependent sources as well.
4. Which quantity may be added directly by superposition in a linear network?
    a. a branch voltage;
    b. resistor power;
    c. efficiency;
    d. temperature rise.
5. A Thévenin equivalent at port A-B preserves:
    a. every internal branch current;
    b. internal component temperatures;
    c. the terminal voltage-current relation over the stated range;
    d. behavior at every other possible port.
6. For a fixed positive resistive Thévenin source, $R_L=R_\text{th}$ gives:
    a. maximum load power and zero internal loss;
    b. maximum load power and equal dissipation in the equivalent series
       resistance and load;
    c. maximum voltage regulation and 100% port-delivery efficiency;
    d. minimum load power.

*[Key: 1-b, 2-c, 3-c, 4-a, 5-c, 6-b.]*

**Retrieval and explanation**

1. Define node, branch, path, loop, mesh, port, and reference node. Explain why
   every mesh is a loop but not every loop is a mesh.
2. Starting from @eq-f04-continuity, explain precisely what approximation leads
   to @eq-f04-kcl-lumped. Give one observation that would reveal unrepresented
   charge storage.
3. Explain why @eq-f04-kvl-lumped and @eq-f04-faraday do not conflict. State when
   a localized inductor is sufficient and when coupled branches or an induced
   source are needed to represent external linked flux.
4. A connected network has 7 nodes and 10 branches. How many independent KCL
   equations and independent loop equations exist? *[Self-check: 6 and 4.]*

**Estimation and topology**

5. Without solving @fig-f04-bridge, estimate the sign and scale of $I_{12}$.
   Then predict what changes if $R_5$ is increased tenfold.
6. A $9.0$ V source connects through 1 kΩ to node $x$; 2 kΩ connects $x$ to
   ground, and a 1 mA current source injects into $x$. Estimate whether $v_x$ is
   above or below the ordinary 1 kΩ–2 kΩ divider voltage before calculating.
7. Draw one planar network with three loops but only two meshes. Identify the
   redundant loop equation as an algebraic combination of the mesh equations.

**Derivation and calculation**

8. Re-solve @eq-f04-bridge-node1 and @eq-f04-bridge-node2 by elimination. Calculate
   all five resistor currents with declared directions and show both node
   residuals. *[Self-check: $v_1=6.688$ V, $v_2=6.841$ V,
   $I_{12}=-32.6~\mu\text{A}$.]*
9. Reverse only the reference arrow for $I_{12}$ in the bridge. Which equations
   and numerical signs change, and which physical observations do not?
10. A 4.0 V ideal source lies between unknown nodes $p$ and $q$, positive at
    $p$. Node $p$ connects to 10 V through 2 kΩ; node $q$ connects to ground
    through 3 kΩ. Write the supernode KCL equation and source constraint, solve
    $v_p,v_q$, and recover source current.
11. Re-solve @eq-f04-two-mesh-system. Calculate each physical branch current and
    verify TP1 by three independent voltage paths.
12. A 2 mA current source lies in the branch shared by two clockwise meshes and
    points with mesh 1. Write the current constraint and describe the perimeter
    used for the supermesh KVL equation.
13. Derive @eq-f04-thevenin-line from two safe loaded points
    $(v_1,i_1)$ and $(v_2,i_2)$. Show that
    $R_\text{th}=(v_1-v_2)/(i_2-i_1)$ under the chapter's port reference.
14. Use $(1-x)^2\ge0$ to derive @eq-f04-max-power. Then show that load power is
    half its maximum at two reciprocal normalized resistances, and find them.

**Linearity and equivalents**

15. For @fig-f04-one-port with $R_L=3.00$ kΩ, first use @eq-f04-millman to
    calculate the loaded node voltage directly, then reproduce the two
    superposition contributions. Calculate total load power and show numerically
    why the separate source-contribution powers do not add to it.
16. Find the Thévenin and Norton equivalents seen from A-B if $R_2$ changes to
    4.00 kΩ. Predict the loaded voltage for $R_L=3.00$ kΩ and verify it by direct
    KCL.
17. **Extension.** A linear amplifier output contains a dependent source but no independent
    source after its input is set to zero. Explain why $V_\text{OC}/I_\text{SC}$
    becomes $0/0$ and specify a test-source method for finding output resistance.
18. Give two internal quantities that a Thévenin equivalent cannot predict even
    when it predicts the load voltage exactly. Design one observation that would
    distinguish two internally different but terminally equivalent networks.

**Data interpretation**

19. Recalculate the final column of @tbl-f04-synthetic. Using the stated
    illustrative accuracy limit, decide which rows can share one straight line
    and which cannot. State why this is not a physical qualification.
20. A synthetic bridge test reports TP$_S$ = 10.001 V, TP1 = 6.690 V, and
    TP2 = 6.839 V. Resistors have independently established values equal to their
    labels within ±0.2%, and each voltage has an accuracy limit of ±5 mV. Estimate
    $I_{12}$ and explain which additional bounds are needed before declaring a KCL
    failure.
21. A load sweep curves gradually without a source-state change. Rank resistor
    self-heating, abrupt current limiting, and a fixed voltmeter-loading error as
    hypotheses. Name a discriminating test for the two most plausible.

**Debugging**

22. For @fig-f04-one-port with $R_L=3.00$ kΩ, a student's superposition work
    opens the voltage source and shorts the current source. Explain both errors
    from the zero-valued source relations, then calculate the resulting wrong
    load-voltage contributions and compare their sum with 6.000 V.
23. A node solver reports a singular square system. Give one floating-network
    cause and one equation-selection cause in which a redundant equation replaced
    a required independent constraint. Explain why adding a redundant residual
    check without discarding the independent set does not make the physical
    network indeterminate, and name a test that distinguishes the two causes.
24. A physical bridge gives nearly zero $I_{12}$ although the labelled ratios are
    unbalanced. Propose three competing hypotheses involving topology, component
    value, and instrument intrusion. Identify test points or de-energized checks
    that separate them.
25. A drawn wire crosses another without a junction dot, but the breadboard wires
    are electrically joined. Explain why correct algebra for the drawing still
    fails to predict the hardware and state the smallest schematic repair.

**Open design**

26. Redesign the one-port values so a receiver with
    $2.0~\text{k}\Omega\le R_L\le10~\text{k}\Omega$ receives more than 5.0 V
    while no resistor exceeds 20 mW. State source and resistor tolerances, check
    all relevant corners, include voltmeter loading, and write a guarded acceptance
    rule.
27. Design a safe L03 load sweep that estimates $V_\text{th}$ and
    $R_\text{th}$ without a physical short. Specify loads and power ratings,
    voltage/current references, instruments and ranges, source current limit,
    test points, raw records, uncertainty basis, stop conditions, and the evidence
    that would falsify linearity.
28. Compare two interface goals for the same source: (a) maximum received power
    and (b) output voltage within 2% of open circuit. Derive the load-resistance
    requirement for each and defend which one fits a sensor input. State why a
    maximum-power slogan cannot replace a requirement.

## Connections

Chapter ID: `F04`. Its registered prerequisite is
[F03](f03-sources-loads-resistance.qmd), which supplies source/load operating
points, resistance, loading, and series/parallel reductions. The algebra support
is [M01](../appendices/m01-algebra-units-complex.qmd); matrix methods, when needed
for larger networks, are developed just in time in
[M02](../appendices/m02-calculus-linear-differential.qmd).

The node and loop structure persists when
[F07](f07-capacitors-inductors-transients.qmd) adds storage and when
[F08](f08-sinusoids-impedance-frequency.qmd) adds impedance.
It also feeds [A01](../02-analog/a01-semiconductor-diodes.qmd) and
[A02](../02-analog/a02-transistors.qmd), where nonlinear and controlled branches
enter the same network equations. [F06](f06-measurement-uncertainty-debug.qmd)
provides the uncertainty and fault-isolation discipline needed to turn residuals
into evidence.

The aligned physical evidence is **Lab L03 — Network conservation and
equivalents**, at safety level L1, and the ideas contribute to integrating
milestone **M1**. Reproducible configuration and raw-data practice connects to
[T03](../appendices/t03-reproducible-engineering.qmd). These IDs state learning
and evidence goals; they do not authorize practical work. The
[Part I introduction](index.qmd) and [reading roadmap](../roadmap.qmd) show the
full route.

## References

Claims are cited where used. Charge continuity and the electromagnetic boundary
of loop-voltage reasoning follow canonical field treatments
[@griffiths2017electrodynamics; @ulaby2015electromagnetics]. Linear network
analysis, superposition, dependent-source practice, and one-port equivalents
follow the standard circuit treatment in [@sedra2020microelectronic]. Practical
bench reasoning and the insistence on checking a compact description against the
physical circuit follow [@horowitz2015art]. Measurement and uncertainty language
follows [@jcgm2008gum]. The complete
[bibliography](../references.qmd) collects these sources. No source substitutes
for component datasheets, local procedures, or physical qualification.
